module.title: Getting Started
module.description: Learn FL Studio's core interface and essential setup before you make a single sound. Every skill in this course builds on these foundations.

lessons.title: FL Studio Interface Overview
lessons.body:
When you first open FL Studio, four windows define everything you will do here. Understanding what each one is *for* — before touching any of them — will save you hours of confusion.

## The Four Core Windows

**Toolbar** (the strip at the top of the screen) — transport controls (play, stop, record), the BPM display, time signature, master volume, and master pitch. This bar is always visible regardless of which other windows you have open.

**Channel Rack (F6)** — where you build *patterns*. Each row is one sound source: a drum hit, a synth, a sample. The step sequencer grid on the right lets you trigger each sound at specific points in a bar. Think of it as your loop-building workspace.

**Playlist (F5)** — the arrangement view. Once you have built a pattern in the Channel Rack, you place it here as a block on a timeline. Time moves left to right; each row is a separate track. This is where a collection of loops becomes a full song.

**Mixer (F9)** — where you balance levels and add effects per sound. By default everything routes to the master output — you need individual routing before you can EQ or compress sounds independently.

## The Mental Model That Changes Everything

The Channel Rack builds loops. The Playlist arranges them into a song.

This distinction trips up almost every beginner. You can spend an hour building a great drum pattern in the Channel Rack, but if you never open the Playlist and place that pattern onto a track, your project will never be longer than a single bar. Time in the Channel Rack is *circular* — it loops. Time in the Playlist is *linear* — it is your song.

## Keyboard Shortcuts to Learn Now

| Window | Shortcut |
|---|---|
| Playlist | F5 |
| Channel Rack | F6 |
| Piano Roll | F7 |
| Browser | F8 |
| Mixer | F9 |
| Audio Settings | F10 |

FL Studio is designed to keep all four main windows open simultaneously, either floating or docked. Spend five minutes now arranging them so nothing overlaps, then save this as your default template (Options → Save default template).
keyTakeaways: The Channel Rack builds patterns (loops). The Playlist arranges them into a song. Without placing patterns in the Playlist, a project is just a loop playing in circles.
FL Studio has four core windows — Toolbar, Channel Rack (F6), Playlist (F5), and Mixer (F9) — each with a dedicated keyboard shortcut worth memorising now.
Time in the Channel Rack is circular (looping). Time in the Playlist is linear (your actual song). These are fundamentally different and serve different purposes.
lessons.tryThis: Open FL Studio and press F5, F6, F7, F8, and F9 one at a time. For each window, write one sentence in your own words describing what you see — without looking anything up. After writing all five, compare your descriptions to the explanations above. Where were you right? Where were you surprised?

lessons.title: Setting Up Audio Drivers (ASIO)
lessons.body:
If FL Studio sounds fine through your speakers but you notice a lag between pressing a key and hearing the note — or if the audio stutters and crackles — the problem is almost certainly your audio driver. This lesson fixes that.

## Why Default Drivers Sound Bad

Windows and macOS ship with audio drivers designed for general-purpose use: system sounds, video playback, browser audio. They add a safety buffer — called *latency* — to prevent dropouts. That buffer is fine for watching YouTube. For making music, a 200 ms delay between pressing a pad and hearing the kick drum destroys your ability to play in time.

The solution is a low-latency audio driver: **ASIO** on Windows, **CoreAudio** on Mac.

## ASIO on Windows

ASIO (Audio Stream Input/Output) is a driver protocol developed by Steinberg that bypasses the Windows audio stack entirely and talks directly to your audio hardware.

**If you have an audio interface** (Focusrite Scarlett, PreSonus AudioBox, etc.): install the ASIO drivers from the manufacturer's website. These are purpose-built for your hardware and will give the lowest latency.

**If you don't have an audio interface**: install **ASIO4ALL** — a free wrapper that applies ASIO-style low-latency buffering to your built-in sound card. It won't match a dedicated interface, but it is dramatically better than the Windows defaults.

ASIO4ALL is free and available from its official site.

## Configuring the Driver in FL Studio

1. Open **Options → Audio Settings** (or press **F10**)
2. Under **Device**, open the dropdown and select your ASIO driver (your interface name, or "ASIO4ALL v2")
3. Click **Show ASIO panel** to open the driver's own control panel
4. Set **Buffer length** — start at **256 samples** and work down

## The Buffer Size Trade-off

| Buffer Size | Latency | CPU Load |
|---|---|---|
| 64–128 samples | ~3–6 ms (excellent) | Higher — can cause dropouts |
| 256 samples | ~12 ms (good) | Balanced — start here |
| 512–1024 samples | ~23–46 ms (acceptable for mixing) | Lower |

**Rule of thumb**: Use the lowest buffer size that does not produce clicks or dropouts while you are recording. When mixing without recording live, raise the buffer to free up CPU.

## Checking Your Latency

After selecting a driver, FL Studio shows **Input latency** and **Output latency** values at the bottom of the Audio Settings panel. Output latency under 15 ms is comfortable for live playing. Over 30 ms will feel noticeably sluggish.

## macOS

macOS uses CoreAudio natively — FL Studio detects it automatically. You still need to set the buffer size in the same Audio Settings panel (F10). Start at 256 samples and lower until you hit dropouts, then back off one step.
keyTakeaways: Default Windows audio drivers add 100–200 ms of latency — too slow for live playing. ASIO bypasses the Windows audio stack and brings latency under 15 ms.
If you don't own a dedicated audio interface, ASIO4ALL gives you ASIO-level low latency on your built-in sound card for free.
Buffer size is a trade-off: lower buffer = less latency but higher CPU load. Start at 256 samples and find the lowest value that runs without clicks on your machine.
lessons.tryThis: Open FL Studio's Audio Settings (F10). Note your current output latency number. Switch to your ASIO driver (or ASIO4ALL), set the buffer to 256 samples, and note the new latency value. Now try 128 samples — does it play cleanly, or do you hear clicks? Find the lowest buffer size that runs without dropouts and leave it there.
